title: Zara's Story
story_html:
Zara was a quiet student who usually kept to herself. She lived in a small, quiet town, but she never let that stop her from dreaming big. One warm afternoon, her teacher invited the whole class to write a story for the local newspaper's Young Writers contest.

At first, Zara did not want to enter. She felt her writing was nothing special, and she did not expect to win against so many other kids. But her best friend gave her a simple reason to try: "You love telling stories. Isn't that enough?"

That evening, Zara walked to the edge of a hill near her house to think. The view from up there was wide and clear, with wild flowers growing along the path. She took a deep breath of the sweet evening air, then sat down to write about her favorite memory: a family trip to a similar hill, long ago.

Writing felt like the first step toward something new. Zara worked for hours, stopping only to stretch her tired arms. She wrote about a poor but happy family who never let hard times make them forget how much joy they could find together, in small, clean moments.

A few weeks later, her teacher called her name in class. It seemed almost too odd to be true: Zara's story had won second prize, and the editors decided to publish it the very next week!

Zara felt so grateful that day. She realized winning was not the only treat — writing itself had become something she truly loved, and even the shy, active kids in the back clapped loudly. As the sun began to shine through the classroom window, Zara smiled, knowing this was just possible after all, and rather better than she had ever imagined.

step.def: A movement made by lifting one foot and putting it down.

student.def: A person who is learning at a school.

want.def: To wish to have or do something.

sweet.def: Having a pleasant, sugar-like taste or feeling.

similar.def: Almost the same as something else.

expect.def: To think that something will probably happen.

prize.def: Something won for doing well, or a reward.

reason.def: Why something happens or is done.

warm.def: A little hot; having a comfortable amount of heat.

invite.def: To ask someone to come to a place or event.

publish.def: To prepare and print a book so people can read it.

possible.def: Able to happen or be done.

edge.def: The outer part or border of something.

treat.def: Something special given to make someone happy.

newspaper.def: Printed sheets with news, published every day.

wild.def: Living in nature, not at home.

shine.def: To give off a bright light.

view.def: What you can see from a place.

stretch.def: To make your body or a part of it longer or straighter.

questions.qtext: In "At first, Zara did not want to enter. She felt her writing was nothing special," what does want mean?
options: to be forced to do something
to wish or desire to do something
questions.explain: B — want means to wish to do something, and here Zara did not wish to enter the contest.

questions.qtext: In "It seemed almost too odd to be true: Zara's story had won second prize," what does odd mean?
questions.explain: B — Zara could barely believe the news, showing that it felt strange and surprising to her.

questions.qtext: In "Zara worked for hours, stopping only to stretch her tired arms," what does stretch mean?
options: to fold her arms and rest
to make her arms longer and looser after sitting still
questions.explain: B — after sitting and writing for a long time, Zara stretched her arms to loosen them up.

questions.qtext: Why did Zara decide to write her story about a poor but happy family?
options: Because her teacher told her exactly what to write about
Because she wanted to write about a family who found joy in small, clean moments together
Because she could not think of any other idea
Because the contest required a sad ending
questions.explain: B — Zara chose to write about a family that stayed joyful together despite hard times, inspired by her own memories.

questions.qtext: How did Zara feel by the end of the story?
options: Disappointed that she did not win first prize
Grateful and happy, realizing that writing itself mattered more than winning
Nervous about reading her story out loud
Angry that her classmates did not believe her
questions.explain: B — Zara felt grateful and realized that loving to write was the real treat, even more than winning the prize.
